chunks.title: College of Engineering Computer Science and Engineering CSCE 632 Syllabus
chunks.content:
College of Engineering
Computer Science and Engineering
CSCE 632 Syllabus
Section 600 (54784)
Accessible Computing
Spring 2026 - College Station

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Michael Moore
Email: jmichael@tamu.edu

Phone: 979-845-5475
Office Hours
Make an Appointment
Preferred Contact Method

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content:
Accessible Computing. (3-0). Credit 3. Exploration of traditionally disenfranchised
user populations due to disability including discrimination and population
characteristics; review of ethical and legal motivations for creating accessible
technology; universal design and its role in the development, evaluation, design,
and implementation of equitable and inclusive software and computer-based
solutions; application of accessibility standards; compilation of a literature review
on a current topic in accessible computing. Prerequisites: Graduate classification.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Graduate classification.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

• Analyze common classes of disabilities with respect to culture, identity, and
typical challenges in digital environments, and propose appropriate
mitigation strategies.
• Justify the ethical and practical need for accessibility to employers and clients
using evidence-based arguments and effective communication strategies.
• Interpret and evaluate key legal frameworks related to disability and
accessibility, and assess their implications for technology development.
• Compare and critique different models of disability, including the civil rights
model, and analyze their impact on approaches to accessibility.
• Apply and evaluate relevant accessibility standards in the design and
implementation of technology solutions.
• Assess facilitators and barriers to technology use, and design or
recommend strategies that reduce barriers and support accessibility for
diverse users.
• Develop accessible technologies, documents, and presentations that comply
with established standards, and evaluate their effectiveness for people with
disabilities as well as other populations.
• Critique technology, identifying instances where accessibility standards may
be insufficient, and propose design alternatives that fulfill the underlying
goals of accessibility.
• Design, implement, and evaluate solutions that enable equitable
interaction, mediating challenges and barriers for specific disabilities, and
justify design choices within the framework of universal design principles.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: None

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Graded work modifications
If you want to challenge any grading, you must do so within one week of when the
grade is published.

We reserve the right to audit the grades for any assignments submitted to this
course. During the audit process, we can decrease or increase your score. This
could result in lowering the score of already released grades.

chunks.title: Grade Weighting
chunks.content:
Pre-Class Engagement (CSCE 432: 10%, CSCE 632: 5%)

Pre-class engagement can include but is not limited to course readings, quizzes,
polls, and surveys.

• You get credit by submitting engagement submissions prior to the start of
class.
• At the end of the semester, we will calculate your completion rate and add
15% capped at 100%.
• This allows you to miss some pre-class engagements without negatively
impacting your score. For example, a friend has a personal crisis, and
you choose to console them instead of doing some pre-class
engagements. You do not have to ask the instructor for special
consideration since it is built into the course.

chunks.title: Group Class Activities (10%)
chunks.content:
You will work with people at your table or move to a table so that there are at least
3 people per group.

• You will get credit if you attend class and you provide what is required for the
activity.
• At the end of the semester, we will calculate your completion rate and add
15% capped at 100%.
• This allows you to miss some group class activities without negatively
impacting your score. For example, you had a flat tire on the way to
campus that caused you to miss a group class activity. You do not have
to ask the instructor for special consideration since it is built into the
course.

chunks.title: Assignments (25%)
chunks.content:
You will complete assignments throughout the semester either individually or with
others as specified in the assignment. Note that assignments done with others are
not a part of the group project.

• Some assignments will be peer graded.
1. Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your evaluation
of other student assignments.
■ You can lose points for grading significantly differently than your
peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You will need to
grade critically rather than give everyone full credit.)

chunks.title: Quick Applications (20%)
chunks.content:
Timed assignments that have you do quick application of class materials. They are
designed to take 15 to 20 minutes while you will have at least 45 minutes to
complete them.

chunks.title: Literature Review (CSCE 432: 0%, CSCE 632: 10%)
chunks.content:
Students will compile a literature review on an accessibility topic comprised of at
least 10 related peer reviewed conference or journal papers.

The literature review will clearly lay out
• themes and gaps in the research supported by evidence from the papers
• an annotated bibliography of each paper cited in the review

chunks.title: Current Research Presentation (CSCE 432: 10%, CSCE 632: 5%)
chunks.content:
You will present a summary of HCI research.

• Section
• 432: Your presentation will be based on a recent paper from a refereed
source related to disability/accessibility.
• 632: Your presentation will be based on your Literature Review (See
above.).
• Your presentation will be peer graded.
• Grading
• This will be peer graded.
■ Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your
evaluation of other student assignments.
■ You can lose points for grading significantly differently than
your peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You
will need to grade critically rather than give everyone full
credit.)

chunks.title: Team Project (25%)
chunks.content:
You will work with a group to address a barrier encountered by people with
disabilities that does not have an acceptable solution. Your team's barrier must be
approved by the instructor. Teams must design and implement a technology based
solution that provides equitable interactions for the barrier.

• Parts of this will be peer graded.
1. Your score will be based on peer evaluations as well as your evaluation
of other student assignments.
■ You can lose points for grading significantly differently than your
peers or giving all students the same scores. (E.g. You will need to
grade critically rather than give everyone full credit.)
• The instructor will establish a multiplier for you based on your team peer
evaluations.
1. The instructor can modify the multiplier at their discretion.
For example, if you are not actively participating with your group
and you are assigned a multiplier of 0.9 and your group's final
project score is 90, then your score would become 0.9*90 which is
81.

chunks.title: Grading Scale
chunks.content:
• Grades will be rounded to the nearest whole number.
• Grades will be assigned according to the following scale:
• A: ≥ 90
○ B: < 90 and ≥ 80
○ C: < 80 and ≥ 70
○ D: < 70 and ≥ 60
• F: < 60
• These grades represent varying degrees of achievement (see the university's
grading system):
• A: excellent
• B: good
• C: satisfactory
• D: passing
• F: failing

chunks.title: Instructor Modifications
chunks.content:
• If warranted, the course instructor may assign a failing grade regardless
of a student's computed final score.
• Failing to complete work can lead to letter grade reductions even if
having a zero would still mathematically result in a higher letter grade.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
General G
General Guidelines
• It is your responsibility to keep up with the class, even when unexpected
events interfere.
• All requests for extensions due to excused absences must follow the process
for documenting absences below.
• Penalties for unexcused late submissions are 5% per calendar day, unless
specified otherwise.
• Each student/team has a limited number of "late days" they may use by
request as described below.

chunks.title: Late Days Table
chunks.content:
Summary of assignment types, late day allocations and limits.

| Assignment Type | Penalty for Late Work | Late Days Allotted | Max Late Days/Assignment | Late Day Request Process | Excused Absence Extension Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Assignments | 5% per calendar day | 6/student | 2 | Submit request via link on course Canvas homepage | Submit documentation via link on course Canvas homepage |
| Team Project | 5% per calendar day | 10/team | 2 | Submit request via link on course Canvas homepage | Discuss with instructor |
| Quick Applications | 5% per calendar day | 3/student | 2 | Submit request via link on course Canvas homepage | Submit documentation via link on course Canvas homepage |
| Pre-Class Engagement | Not accepted late | N/A | N/A | N/A | Submit documentation via link on course Canvas homepage |
| Group Class Activities | Not accepted late | N/A | N/A | N/A | Submit documentation via link on course Canvas homepage |
| Current Research Presentation | Not accepted late | N/A | N/A | N/A | Submit documentation via link on course Canvas homepage |
| Literature Review | Not accepted late | N/A | N/A | N/A | Submit documentation v link on course Canvas homepage |

chunks.title: Submitting Late Work
chunks.content:
1. Submit late work as usual.
• Do not wait for approval of an excused absence since you might miss
the extended deadline while you wait.
2. Submit a late day request (after submission) via the appropriate link on the
course Canvas homepage.
3. Follow the process for documenting absences.
• Documentation accepted as excused will trigger the removal of any
penalty already assessed.
4. For team projects, late days are limited per team, not per individual.

chunks.title: Documenting Absences
chunks.content:
• Must be documented via the Absence Documentation link on the course
Canvas homepage.
• Do not send email. Instead put what you would have put in the email
into a simple document and submit that.
• Note that documentation can be a rationale that you type into a document
and submit.
• For example, you can say "I was sick".
Further medical documentation will be needed if you are sick too
frequently.
• Excused absences may receive an extension for graded work.
○ Note that the instructor has the discretion to approve an excused
absence when it does not fall within the automatic excused absences
defined by the university.
• Teams are generally expected to complete work on time, even if a team
member has an excused absence.
• Discuss team extensions for extenuating circumstances with the
instructor.

chunks.title: Unexcused Absences:
chunks.content:
• Standard late penalties apply.
• Late days may be used up to the maximum outlined.

chunks.title: Extenuating Circumstances
chunks.content:
For any questions or special circumstances, contact the instructor through Canvas,
the class chat, office hours, or make an appointment.

chunks.title: Technology Requirements
chunks.content:
• Personal computer that fulfills the Texas A&M Computer Requirements.
• Used to consume content and interact with online course resources.
• Able to connect your computer to project via an HDMI cable.
• You will need an HDMI adapter if your device does not have an HDMI
port.
• Wireless device to submit online forms during class.
• Cell phones and laptop computers are common options.
• Technology to Capture an Image
• At times you may be asked to create a sketch or diagram to submit
electronically. You need the ability to get the diagram into a format that
can be uploaded. You could sketch on paper and take a picture with
your camera, sketch on a tablet device, or use a scanner. You should be
able to do this quickly, especially if needed for an online timed
submission.

chunks.title: Accessibility Statement
chunks.content:
Howdy! Your CSCE 489 instructors want you to succeed in this challenging course.
While the Disability Accommodation Portal (AIM Portal) makes it easier to share
accommodation information, we ask that you also schedule a meeting with your
section's instructor to discuss your accommodations in person. In addition to
accommodations, we're happy to talk about strategies for succeeding in this
course.

We strongly encourage you to secure your accommodations—even if you're
unsure you'll need them. When Dr. Moore began teaching at Texas A&M, he
noticed a concerning pattern: students often chose to take the first exam without
using their approved accommodations. Many said they wanted to “try it without
them." After the first exam, there was often a rush of accommodation requests.
These instances have decreased significantly since we've been more explicit about
our preferences.

While Dr. Moore suspects that some students hesitate due to negative experiences
with previous instructors, we want you to know that our department culture is one
of support. We are committed to providing accommodations approved by
Disability Resources with professionalism and care.

"We prefer that you get your accommodations and not need them, rather than
trying without your accommodations and finding out that you needed them.”

Finally, everyone should be mindful of creating software and digital artifacts that
are accessible. If you're majoring in computer science or computer engineering,
consider taking CSCE 432: Accessible Computing, a course developed by Dr. Moore
to help students design with accessibility in mind.

chunks.title: Professionalism and Respect
chunks.content:
You are expected to treat your instructor and all other participants in the course
with courtesy and respect. Your comments to others should be factual,
constructive, and free from harassing statements. You are encouraged to disagree
with other students, but such disagreements need to be based upon facts and
documentation (rather than prejudices and personalities).

Professional and respectful engagement is expected at all times; failure to do so
may impact assignment grades.

Students need to contribute in intelligent, positive, and constructive manners
within the course. Behaviors that are abusive, disruptive, or harassing may result in
disciplinary actions as specified within the Student Rules.

Sensitive or controversial topics might be discussed in this course. Comments
posted within this course are to remain confidential to the audience of this course;
do not copy or share messages or writings from this course with others not in this
course section.

Students in this course are also responsible for being familiar with the University.
student rules and policies.

chunks.title: Spirit & Intent
chunks.content:
Assignments are designed to help you engage with HCl concepts. Doing anything to
subvert the learning objectives harm your learning experience and constitute
academic dishonesty.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
With the rise of artificial intelligence (Al) technologies, our creative processes are
evolving. However, these tools do not replace the essential skills of drafting,
revising, and reflecting on texts, programs, and lectures. Nor do they substitute for
the critical thinking required to locate, evaluate, incorporate, and cite sources
responsibly.

Education is about preparing future innovators and leaders. That includes learning
to use emerging technologies ethically and effectively. In this course, you are
permitted to use generative Al tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Google Bard, DALL·E 2) to assist
with assignments if you choose to. These tools can help you brainstorm ideas or
provide feedback, but they should not complete your work for you.

To maintain academic integrity:
• Cite any Al-generated content that informs your work. This includes in-text
citations, quotations, and entries in your reference list.
• Failure to properly attribute Al-generated material is considered
academic dishonesty.
• No more than 30% of any assignment should consist of Al-generated text.

For guidance on citing generative Al using APA 7th edition, refer to the APA Style
Blog for examples, including how to cite ChatGPT in both the body of your work
and your reference list.

Please be aware of the limitations of generative Al:
• It often recycles existing ideas rather than producing original thought.
• It may present false or misleading information alongside accurate content.
• It can hallucinate—creating citations or facts that do not exist.
• It may restructure your writing in ways that distort your intended meaning.

Always review and critically evaluate any Al-generated content before including it in
your work.

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
You are required to attend every class, arrive on time, and stay the whole time. You
are responsible for learning the material covered in class, regardless of your
attendance.
